coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature synthesis of fluorescent carbon-based nanoparticles and their application in multidimensional sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shuai
Liu, Meng-Xian
Yu, Yong-Liang
Wang, Jian-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Highly sensitive metal ion discrimination and detection are of great value in environmental monitoring. However, it is still a challenge to obtain multidimensional information from a single material by applying various transduction principles to realize high-order sensing. Herein, a kind of yellow luminescent sub-10 nm carbon-based nanoparticles (CNPs) was prepared by chemically oxidative polymerization. The synthesis process involves only mixing the o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and ammonium persulphate (APS) aqueous solutions together and allowing to stand at room temperature for 24 h without any external heating or additional energy input. Due to the rich aromatic diamine groups, these room-temperature carbon-based nanoparticles (RT-CNPs) can chelate various metal ions, leading to the changes in absorption and fluorescence as well as further change of fluorescence with the addition of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Base on this phenomenon, a lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform for discrimination of metal ions has been developed. In both the triple-channel and double-channel modes, eight metal ions, including Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Ni2+, can be discriminated at a low concentration of 0.5 μM. In addition, the sensing platform was also validated by 100% accurate identification of 48 unknown samples and spiked Lake waters.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Room-temperature carbon dots
Multidimensional sensing
Lab-on-a-nanoparticle
Metal ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterizations of the RT-CNPs
Construction of the multi-channel sensing platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation strategy of RT-CNPs and the working mechanism of the sensing platform for discrimination and detection of metal ions.

(A) TEM and (B) AFM images of the RT-CNPs (Insets in TEM image: corresponding size distribution and HRTEM of the RT-CNPs); (C) UV–vis absorption of the RT-CNPs and o-PD, and FL excitation spectrum of the RT-CNPs; (D) FL emission spectra of the RT-CNPs at different excitation wavelengths; (E) FTIR and (F) Raman spectra of the RT-CNPs.

(A) UV–vis absorption and (B) FL spectra of 10 μg mL−1 RT-CNPs in the presence of 2 μM different metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Ni2+); (C) FL spectra of 10 μg mL−1 RT-CNPs in the presence of 2 μM different metal ions and 0.2 mM TMB; (D) UV–vis absorption and (E) FL titration experiments of 10 μg mL−1 RT-CNPs in the presence of different metal ions at various concentrations (0–20 μM); (F) FL titration experiment of 10 μg mL−1 RT-CNPs in the presence of different metal ions at various concentrations (0–20 μM) and 0.2 mM TMB.

(A) Heat plot of the response fingerprints of 10 μg mL−1 RT-CNPs in the presence of 0.5 μM metal ions; (B) The most important contributors in the original triple-channel (FL1-Abs-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform; (C) The three-dimensional principal component analysis (PCA) plot for 0.5 μM metal ions by the triple-channel (FL1-Abs-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform; The two-dimensional PCA scatter plots for (D) 0.5 μM and (E) 5 μM metal ions by the simplified double-channel (FL1-Abs) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform; (F) The two-dimensional PCA scatter plot for 0.5 μM metal ions by the simplified double-channel (FL1-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform.
PCA plots for various concentrations of (A) Hg2+ and (C) Co2+ by triple-channel lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform, and linear relationships for the concentrations of (B) Hg2+ and (D) Co2+.

(A) PCA plot for the discrimination of Hg2+, Co2+, and their mixture at a total concentration of 0.5 μM; (B) PCA plot for the discrimination of different metal ions (0.5 μM) in lake water by using the triple-channel lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform, Qinghai Lake.

head.ce:title: Room-temperature synthesis of fluorescent carbon-based nanoparticles and their application in multidimensional sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110169, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors have equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly sensitive metal ion discrimination and detection are of great value in environmental monitoring. However, it is still a challenge to obtain multidimensional information from a single material by applying various transduction principles to realize high-order sensing. Herein, a kind of yellow luminescent sub-10 nm carbon-based nanoparticles (CNPs) was prepared by chemically oxidative polymerization. The synthesis process involves only mixing the o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and ammonium persulphate (APS) aqueous solutions together and allowing to stand at room temperature for 24 h without any external heating or additional energy input. Due to the rich aromatic diamine groups, these room-temperature carbon-based nanoparticles (RT-CNPs) can chelate various metal ions, leading to the changes in absorption and fluorescence as well as further change of fluorescence with the addition of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Base on this phenomenon, a lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform for discrimination of metal ions has been developed. In both the triple-channel and double-channel modes, eight metal ions, including Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Ni2+, can be discriminated at a low concentration of 0.5 μM. In addition, the sensing platform was also validated by 100% accurate identification of 48 unknown samples and spiked Lake waters.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metals bring serious threats to human health due to their high toxicity, high solubility, non-biodegradability, and high bioaccumulation in human body and food chains [1,2]. With the rapid development of various manufacturing industries, more and more heavy metal pollution has pervaded many parts of the world, especially in developing countries [3,4]. Therefore, it is of great importance to selectively and sensitively monitor metal ion contamination in the environment. Standard laboratory methods give accurate measurements of metal ions by using atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), atomic emission spectrometry (AES), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS), and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5,6]. However, these methods are subject to high operating costs, complex operating procedures, and time-consuming limitations [7,8].

By mimicking the mammalian olfaction, “chemical nose” type multidimensional sensing approach has emerged and gained increasing popularity over the past few years. The unique “fingerprint” generated by output data pattern can be used to distinguish analytes by statistical analysis. This technique is an effective and powerful tool for multi-analyte discrimination and detection, especially in complex mixtures [9]. Additionally, their multiple measurements can improve resolving power, accuracy and reproducibility [10,11]. The most common strategy for constructing multidimensional sensing platform is to combine various cross-reactive colorimetric or ﬂuorometric indicators to form a sensor array [12–14]. So far, various sensing platforms have been successfully applied to the discrimination of metal ions, including functionalized ionic microgel sensor array, [15] catechol dyes-based chemosensor array [16], DNA-based array [17,18], and five different carbon nanodots-based array [19]. However, due to the difficulty of molecular design, the high-cost chemical synthesis of recognition elements, and the huge number of testing samples still hindered the large-scale use of sensor arrays. In contrast, the lab-on-a-molecule/nanoparticle approach was proposed by integrating ﬂuorescent, phosphorescent, light-scattering, and/or absorbing signal reporters on a molecule or a nanoparticle [8,20]. This approach requires fewer sensor units, accomplishes multi-measurements in one solution, and improves convenience. However, materials with multidimensional information are rare and difficult to synthesize. Therefore, it is still a challenge to develop new materials with different transduction principles for high-order sensing. Currently, only a few reports are dedicated to lab-on-a-molecule/nanoparticle platform [21–23].

Carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles (CNPs), such as carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and polymer dots (PDs), have aroused much attention owing to their stable fluorescence (FL) properties, chemical inertness and good biocompatibility. Lin et al. demonstrated for the first time the potential applications of CDs for sensing metal ions in “lab-on-a-nanoparticle” approach [24]. However, there was only a short mention of lab-on-CDs, and a detection limit of 100 mM is insufficient to discriminate metal ions at concentrations lower than the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) standards. O-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as a common carbon source, has been used to synthesize carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials by hydrothermal treatment and microwave-assisted synthesis. Although some approaches are facile and/or rapid, they still have many limitations, such as high-energy consumption, complex equipment, low yields, and difficulty in purification.

In this paper, we propose an extremely convenient chemical oxidative polymerization synthesis approach without any extra energy supply or complicated equipment. The only step required was to mix o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) and ammonium persulphate (APS) aqueous solutions, leave them for 24 h, and then redistribute the precipitate into water to form carbon-based nanoparticle solution (Fig. 1 ). These room-temperature prepared carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles (also referred to room-temperature CNPs, and abbreviated as RT-CNPs) have abundant functional groups and exhibit excellent yellow luminescent properties. More interestingly, the absorption spectra can be changed by metal ions. Besides, the FL of as-prepared RT-CNPs can be quenched by metal ions and partially recovered in the presence of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). Herein, we designed a multidimensional (triple-channel) sensing platform for the first time using the RT-CNPs to simultaneously discriminate and detect various metal ions (Fig. 1). In this lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform, eight kinds of metal ions, including Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Ni2+, were chosen as analytes to evaluate the sensing performance. Principal component analysis (PCA) is employed to process the data. Meanwhile, the triple-channel lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform can be simplified to a double-channel sensing platform in the PCA process. Both the triple- and double-sensing modes are applied for the highly discriminable and sensitive detection of metal ions at 0.5 μM.

The RT-CNPs were prepared by a simple room-temperature chemical oxidative polymerization method. Briefly, 54.07 mg of o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) was first dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water in a beaker. Then, 5 mL of 0.1 M ammonium persulphate (APS) was added into the above solution at room temperature, and a noticeable color change was observed immediately (Fig. S1). After standing at room temperature (25 °C) for 24 h, the newly formed precipitate at the bottom of the beaker was collected and washed three times with deionized water. Finally, the obtained RT-CNPs were redistributed by ultrasonication into deionized water for further use.

50 μL of 10 μg mL−1 RT-CNPs and 10 μL of 0.1 μM metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Ni2+) were add into a 1.5-mL centrifuge tube. The mixture was incubated for 30 min at room temperature (25 °C). The relative FL variation ((I0-I)/I0) at 550 nm was measured by using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer, and absorption response (A-A0) at 205 nm was obtained on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer. I0/A0 and I/A represent the signals of RT-CNPs in the absence and presence of metal ions. Afterwards, 20 μL of TMB solution was added into the above mixture at a final concentration of 0.2 mM to record the relative FL variation ((I0-I)/I0) of the partly recovered RT-CNPs at 550 nm, where I0 and I are the FL intensities of RT-CNPs at 550 nm in the absence and presence of metal ions.

This procedure was repeated to produce six replicates of each metal ion. Eight different metal ions were tested six times against the triple-channel signals to give a training data matrix of 3 signals × 8 metal ions × 6 replicates. The data matrix was processed by using principal component analysis (PCA).

48 unknown samples were identified in the blind trial. Each sample contained a metal ion at a concentration of 0.5 μM. The results were analyzed by using the triple-channel (FL1-Abs-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform to identify metal ions.

To evaluate the practicality of this lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform, we collected Lake water from the Qinghai Lake, Caka Salt Lake and Shenyang South Lake in China as real samples. Prior to analysis, each lake water was filtered by using a 0.22 μm membrane, and diluted 100-fold with deionized water for further use. Lake water was spiked with different metal ions (Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+ Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, and Ni2+, all at a concentration of 0.5 μM). The analysis process was similar to the above-described sensing procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of the RT-CNPs
ce:section.ce:para: The new FL RT-CNPs can be easily prepared by a room temperature procedure. As shown in Fig. S1, when o-PD and APS were mixed together, the aqueous solution immediately turned black and then changed to orange. After standing at room temperature for 24 h, some precipitate appeared at the bottom of the bottle, i.e., RT-CNPs.

The morphologies of the as-prepared RT-CNPs were firstly characterized by TEM and AFM. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the RT-CNPs exhibit a narrow size distribution ranging from 3.0 to 6.0 nm in diameter and an average diameter of ca. 5.1 nm. MALDI-TOF mass spectrum of the RT-CNPs (Fig. S1) shows the highest peak at m/z of 821 and a small peak at m/z of 1030. The distinct crystalline lattice spaces with 0.21 nm are observed from the HRTEM image, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphitic carbon. The AFM image (Fig. 2B) further reveals that the height of RT-CNPs is ca. 1.1 nm, indicating that the RT-CNPs consist of one to three layers of graphene-like sheets.

The redistributed RT-CNPs solution showed bright yellow fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp. The UV–vis absorption spectra of RT-CNPs and the raw material (o-PD) are displayed in Fig. 2C. The RT-CNPs exhibit two absorption bands at 260 and 420 nm, respectively assigned to the π–π* transition of the aromatic CC bond, and the n–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 platform containing CN bond [25,26]. In comparison with the absorption bands of o-PD at 209 nm and 294 nm, the significant red-shifts of the electronic absorption transitions in RT-CNPs indicate that the newly formed carbon-based nanomaterials should contain π-conjugated platform with smaller electronic band gaps.

The FL excitation spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2C (blue line). The maximum excitation peak is at 410 nm, corresponding to the absorption band at 420 nm. The RT-CNPs show an excitation-independent feature with maximum FL emission at 550 nm (Fig. 2D), and the maximum emission shows no shift when the excitation wavelength is increased from 350 to 470 nm. To improve RT-CNPs products, the effect of APS and o-PD concentration ratio was studied in detail. The corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra and FL intensities are displayed in Fig. S2. When the concentration ratio is 1:1, the FL intensity and the absorption at 420 nm are the highest. As the concentration ratio increases or decreases, both FL intensity and absorption decline sharply. However, the absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) remains unchanged (11.11%), indicating that the yield of RT-CNPs generated in the ratio of 1:1 is highest.

In order to obtain further insight into the element composition and surface chemical state of the RT-CNPs, FTIR, Raman, and XPS spectra were carried out. As shown in Fig. 2E, the RT-CNPs contain the chemical groups of NH (3375.7 and 3193.3 cm−1), CN (1627 cm−1), CC (1530 cm−1), and CN (1368 and 1237 cm−1). Obviously, there are many nitrogen-containing groups, polyaromatic structures and phenazine-based units on the surface of the RT-CNPs [27]. The graphitic structure is also confirmed by Raman spectrum (Fig. 2F), where the signals of G band at 1558 cm−1 and D band at 1364 cm−1 are assigned to graphitic and disordered structures of carbon materials, respectively. The ratio of G to D band is greater than one, indicating that the RT-CNPs are highly graphitized [28]. The full range XPS spectrum in Fig. S3A confirms that the RT-CNPs consist of C, N, and O elements. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S3B) shows three peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.9 eV, and 288.4 eV, which are assigned to the CC/CC, CN/CO, and CO bands [29,30]. The high resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S3C) can be divided into three peaks at 398.3, 399.3, and 400.1 eV, corresponding to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S3D) confirms the presence of CO (531.7 eV) and CO (533.1 eV) groups. It is concluded that the RT-CNPs possess a large sp2 domain and their surface is linked with an abundance of chemical groups [6].

Finally, the FL stability of RT-CNPs at different pH values was also investigated, which is especially important for the preparation of CDs by a room temperature method. As shown in Fig. S4A, B, the FL intensity decreased in low-pH solutions (pH = 2–6), but remained stable in neutral and weakly alkaline solutions (pH = 6–12). In fact, the FL intensity of the RT-CNPs hardly changed within 10 days (Fig. S4C). Above all, the sub-10 nm RT-CNPs with stable and high FL can be facilely prepared at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the multi-channel sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: We speculate that the RT-CNPs can be used to determine metal ions by virtue of the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of RT-CNPs. In addition, aromatic diamine groups can also play the role of adsorption and chelation of metal ion [31]. As demonstrated by the FTIR spectrum and XPS analysis, abundant oxygen-containing and aromatic diamine groups are present on the surface of RT-CNPs. These functional groups can bind to certain metal ions, thereby affecting the molecular structure of RT-CNPs and their FL and absorption performance.

Initially, the interaction of the RT-CNPs with various metal ions (i.e., Hg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, and Fe3+) was revealed by the changes in absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 A, S5, when metal ions were added into the RT-CNPs solution, the absorption spectrum showed a significant charge-transfer absorption band at 205 nm, indicating the formation of metal-RT-CNPs complex [32]. The molar absorption coefficient of the charge-transfer absorption band is several orders of magnitude higher than π–π* transition at 260 nm and n–π* transition at 420 nm (Fig. S5B), providing a higher sensitivity for sensing applications. Therefore, the absorption titration experiments of metal ions were investigated by measuring the difference in absorbance at 205 nm (A - A0) as response signal. As shown in Fig. 3D, it was observed that different metal ions have various binding abilities to RT-CNPs, i.e., Hg2+ > Co2+ > Pb2+ > Cu2+ > Cd2+ > Cr3+ > Ni2+ > Fe3+.

The addition of metal ions can also induce the FL quenching of RT-CNPs (Fig. 3B, E). The titration isotherm shows the discriminative quenching abilities of metal ions, i.e., Hg2+ > Co2+ > Pb2+ > Cu2+ > Cd2+ > Cr3+ > Fe3+ > Ni2+. The FL quenching data were further analyzed by the modified Stern–Volmer equation, [33,34] and the corresponding binding constants (Ksv) were obtained in Fig. S6. Compared with other metal ions, Hg2+ displayed a maximum Ksv value of 1.2 × 107, indicating that the binding ability was the strongest. The discriminable binding effect depends on the specific chemical groups surrounding the RT-CNPs. TMB as a common chemical contains two free -NH2 groups on the benzidine molecule, and cannot react with the RT-CNPs (Fig. S4D). However, when quantitative TMB solution was added into the mixture of RT-CNPs and metal ions, the FL intensity recovered to some extent due to the competitive binding of metal ions between RT-CNPs and TMB (Fig. 3C, F). This indicates that the metal-ion binding sites of TMB are similar to those of RT-CNPs, i.e., free aromatic diamine groups.

As illustrated in Fig. 1, the discrimination and detection of metal ions were achieved by collecting the absorption (Abs) and two FL (FL1 and FL2, in the presence and absence of TMB) signals. The responses are visually summarized as a heat plot in Fig. 4 A [35]. The fingerprint of every metal ion contains all the information about the effect of metal ion on the RT-CNPs.

PCA is often used as a data processing method for the dimensionality analysis of multidimensional sensing platform. The multidimensional response pattern (3 channels × 8 metal ions × 6 trails) was obtained by sensing eight metal ions through a triple-channel (FL1-Abs-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs platform. After PCA processing, the data matrix was converted into a new set of linearly uncorrelated principal components (PCs) [36]. According to eigenvalues and percentages of the total variances listed in Fig. 4B, it can be concluded that the PC 1 and PC 2 have greater effect on the results. Therefore, the first PC (PC 1, eigenvalue = 2.9) and the second PC (PC 2, eigenvalue = 0.08) were selected to plot the double-dimensional PCA scattergraph as shown in Fig. 4C. PC 1 contributes 96.7% of the total variability in the data, whereas PC 2 accounts for 2.5%. Therefore, the first two PCs contribute ca. 99.2% (>90%) of the total variability. In the scattergraph, six plots of the same color represent six repeated trials for each metal ion. No evident intersection between every two groups is observed, suggesting that eight metal ions are discriminated through the multi-channel sensing platform.

The ultimate goal of multidimensional sensing research is to cut down dimensionality and retain the good discrimination performance. An ideal sensor unit should provide the highest contribution to each PC with statistical significance [37]. Factor score as an important parameter is used to assess the contribution of a single factor to the PCs. As shown in Fig. 4B, FL1 channel has the highest contribution (25.1%) to PC 2, and Abs channel contributes -98.7% to PC 1. Considering the high contribution of Abs and FL1 channels and the convenience of the experimental operation without TMB, the first two channels (i.e., Abs and FL1) were chosen to design a simplified sensing platform. Then, the reconstructed double-channel (Abs and FL1) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform was applied to distinguish eight metal ions. As shown in Fig. 4D, there is no evident intersection among the data groups in the PCA scattergraph, which means that double-channel sensing platform can discriminate eight kinds of 0.5 μM metal ions. This simplified sensor platform can also successfully discriminate metal ions at higher concentrations (1 or 5 μM) (Fig. S7, 4E). Obviously, the contribution of FL2 channel to PC 2 (-9.5%) is only slightly lower than the contribution of Abs channel (-13%), and the contribution to PC 1 is almost the same as that of Abs channel. Considering the convenience of the experimental operation only in the FL detection mode, the double-channel (FL1-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4F, eight metal ions were also successfully discriminated by the double-channel (FL1-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform.

Herein, the lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform for eight metal ions was achieved by triple or double channel with a discrimination limit of 0.5 μM. The discrimination limit for five metal ions is far less than maximum concentrations of most metal ions in the U.S. EPA standard listed in Table S1. The discrimination capability of the present sensing platform is significantly improved when compared to other “Lab-on-a-nanoparticle” sensing systems, and comparable with the multiple sensor array approaches (Table S2). More importantly, the detection process for each analyte in the present sensing platform can be carried out in one solution, while the sensor array platform requires multiple solution preparation. In addition, the sensing process can be performed on the double-channel (FL1-Abs) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform without the assistance of TMB. In this mode, the RT-CNPs are the only required sensing reagent. Alternatively, only fluorescence spectrophotometer is employed for constructing the double-channel (FL1-FL2) lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform. In this mode, the requirement of the instrument is the lowest.

The lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform was further investigated with concentration dependence to different metal ions, as shown in Fig. 5 . The sensing platform shows obvious responses within the concentration ranges of 0–2.0 μM for Hg2+ (Fig. 5A) and 0–5.0 μM for Co2+ (Fig. 5C). A linear relationship between PC 1 and ion concentration is within the ranges of 0-0.2 μM (Hg2+, Fig. 5B) and 0-0.5 μM (Co2+, Fig. 5D). The detection limits of the RT-CNPs-based sensing platform for metal ions are at a nanomolar-level.

To test the robustness of the lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform, 48 unknown samples were analyzed in the blind trial. Samples contain one of eight metal ions in deionized water at a concentration of 0.5 μM. The unknown samples were arranged in terms of Mahalanobis distance to the groups and returned the nearest samples to the respective groups. As listed in Table S3, 48 unknown samples were successfully discriminated and distinguished with an accuracy of 100%.

The mixtures containing Hg2+ and Co2+ at different molar ratios (Hg2+/Co2+ = 100/0, 90/10, 70/30, 50/50, 30/70, 10/90, and 0/100 at the total concentration of 0.5 μM) were chosen for the ensuing experiments. As shown in Fig. 6 A, seven samples are distributed clearly into seven independent clusters in the PCA plot, demonstrating that the discrimination ability of this RT-CNPs-based sensing platform is not sacrificed in the mixture of metal ions.

The anti-interference capability of the developed sensing platform was firstly tested. The common interfering metal ions including K+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Ca2+ were at the concentration of 1 mM. The sensing result illustrated that they are all clearly separated from the metal ions (all at 0.5 μM) as shown in Fig. S9. Then, the applicability of the lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform was demonstrated by identifying eight metal ions spiked in Qinghai Lake water, Caka Salt Lake and South Lake water. As shown in Fig. 6B, S10A&B, the results processed by PCA analysis reveal that these metal ions are clustered into eight different groups without any overlap. The triple-channel lab-on-RT-CNPs sensing platform could discriminate all the samples with identification accuracies of 100%. Thus, the present sensing platform shows a promising application to discriminate metal ions in real samples.

In summary, a novel triple-channel lab-on-a-nanoparticle sensing platform is constructed with a kind of RT-CNPs alone to discriminate and detect metal ions for the first time. The RT-CNPs are prepared through a simple method of chemically oxidative polymerization at room temperature without any additional energy supply or complex equipment. Eight kinds of metal ions are accurately discriminated and detected by the sensing platform at a low concentration of 0.5 μM. Notably, the triple-channel sensing platform can be simplified into a double-channel sensing platform without influencing the sensing accuracy. There are two modes: one is the FL1-Abs channels with RT-CNPs as the only necessary detection reagent, and the other is the FL1-FL2 channels with the only FL instrument. Taking into account the facile synthesis method for RT-CNPs, we believe that this new sensing platform would be widely utilized for extensive analytes with satisfactory performance in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21605014 and 21475018), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N170507001 and N160504010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.03.067.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
